exercises.explain: A standard GAN discriminator categorizes images as real or fake, whereas a Wasserstein GAN critic measures how far apart the real and fake distributions are.

options.text: It measures the distance between data distributions instead of doing binary classification.
It works without any training data.

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between a standard GAN discriminator and a WGAN critic?

cards.body: Imagine training a critic that changes its mind wildly with the smallest tweak. The generator would get chaotic feedback! To prevent this, we need a mathematical speed limit.
cards.title: The Need for Limits
What is 1-Lipschitz?
cards.body: The 1-Lipschitz constraint is a mathematical rule ensuring the critic's output never changes faster than its input. If you change the input image slightly, the score changes only slightly.

cards.title: The Safe Mountain Slope
cards.body: Think of it like a strict hiking trail guide. Instead of sudden, vertical cliffs where you fall infinitely (exploding gradients), the trail has a maximum slope of 45 degrees. It's always a manageable, smooth slope.

cards.title: Small Inputs, Small Output Changes
cards.body: If we change a pixel in an image by a value of 2, a 1-Lipschitz critic is guaranteed to change its rating by at most 2. This prevents the critic from giving wild, extreme feedback.

cards.title: Is Weight Clipping Perfect?
cards.body: You might think weight clipping is the perfect way to enforce this limit. While simple, clamping weights to a strict range like [-0.01, 0.01] often makes the critic learn very slowly or fail to learn complex features.

right.text: Smooth, gradual changes that guide the generator reliably.

left.text: Gradients can explode or vanish, leading to training failure.

exercises.explain: The 1-Lipschitz constraint guarantees that the critic's slope (gradient) is bounded, preventing sudden, wild changes in feedback.
exercises.prompt: Why do we apply the 1-Lipschitz constraint to the WGAN critic?

options.text: It limits how fast the critic's output can change relative to its input.
It forces the generator to produce only one type of image.
It eliminates the need for any neural network weights.

exercises.prompt: Weight clipping is a method used to force the critic to satisfy the 1-Lipschitz constraint.
exercises.explain: Weight clipping is a traditional, simple way to enforce the Lipschitz constraint by keeping weights within a tiny window (like -0.01 to 0.01).
To enforce the Lipschitz constraint, traditional WGANs restrict the network weights to a tiny range using weight clipping.
exercises.sentence: To satisfy the smoothness constraint, traditional WGANs restrict the weight values using weight clipping.
exercises.prompt: To satisfy the smoothness constraint, traditional WGANs restrict the weight values using weight [blank].

options.text: A critic that gives smooth, gradual feedback to guide the generator.
A critic that has extreme, unstable outputs that crash the training.

exercises.prompt: Which behavior shows the successful application of the 1-Lipschitz constraint?
exercises.explain: Without the smoothness constraint, the critic's landscape has wild cliffs, causing gradient explosion. With the constraint, it is smooth, allowing stable training.

exercises.prompt: What is a major downside of using basic weight clipping to enforce the 1-Lipschitz constraint?
exercises.explain: While weight clipping enforces the 1-Lipschitz constraint, it can limit the critic's capacity. In the next lesson, we will explore a better approach: Gradient Penalty.

options.text: It makes the training process run twice as fast.
It can limit the critic's ability to learn complex patterns due to restricted weights.
It completely prevents the generator from updating its weights.

concept: To keep gradients stable, the critic must be mathematically smooth (1-Lipschitz constraint), traditionally enforced by clipping weights to a tiny range.
title: The 1-Lipschitz Constraint
